1: Seven nations, greater and mightier than Israel — the scope of opposition to the covenant people's inheritance is humanly overwhelming. Christ's victory over principalities and powers (Colossians 2:15) is the ultimate disarming of those who oppose his people's inheritance. The seven nations anticipate the principalities and powers Christ faces and defeats in his death and resurrection, clearing the way for his people's advance into the promised inheritance.
2: The total-destruction command (herem) — complete consecration to YHWH of the devoted peoples — is historically bounded divine judgment, not a universalizable military ethic. The NT does not repeat it for the church. Christ's final judgment (Revelation 19:11–21) is the ultimate herem — the final clearing of all that opposes God's purposes — but this belongs to Christ alone, not to his followers (Revelation 19:15).
3: "Do not intermarry with them." The purity of covenant community required boundary-maintenance against religious assimilation. Paul applies the principle spiritually: "do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers" (2 Corinthians 6:14). The boundary is not ethnic but covenantal — the issue is not cultural difference but allegiance to competing lords. Christ draws a boundary around his community defined by faith in him (John 10:26–27), not racial descent.
4: "They will turn your sons away from following me to serve other gods." The mechanism of spiritual danger is social — wrong relationships draw people away from covenant loyalty. Christ gives the same warning about false prophets and wolves in sheep's clothing (Matthew 7:15); Paul warns "bad company corrupts good character" (1 Corinthians 15:33) and "a little leaven leavens the whole lump" (Galatians 5:9). The concern for the spiritual health of the community is consistent across both covenants.
5: "Tear down their altars, shatter their sacred pillars, cut down their Asherah poles." The destruction of idolatrous infrastructure. Christ's healing and exorcism ministry is understood as the advance of the kingdom against the strongholds of the enemy (Matthew 12:28–29). Paul describes the church's mission equivalently: "destroying strongholds... and every lofty opinion raised against the knowledge of God" (2 Corinthians 10:4–5). The altars of the old conquest become the spiritual strongholds of the new.
6: "You are a people holy to the LORD your God... his treasured possession from all the peoples on the face of the earth." The election formula. Peter applies this directly to the church: "you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession" (1 Peter 2:9), combining Exodus 19:6 with Deuteronomy 7:6. The church in Christ is the continuation and expansion of the elect community — election now extended to all nations through faith in the one Israelite who embodied all that Israel was called to be.
7: "The LORD did not set his love on you and choose you because you were more numerous — you were the smallest of all peoples." Election from pure grace, not merit or size. Paul makes the same argument about the new covenant community: "God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise... what is weak to shame the strong" (1 Corinthians 1:27–29). The divine preference for the smallest and least-likely consistently reveals that the power belongs entirely to God, not to human qualification.
8: "Rather, it was because the LORD loved you and was keeping the oath he swore to your fathers." The ground of election: YHWH's love and covenantal faithfulness. Ephesians 1:4–5 traces the church's election to the same pre-existent love: "chosen in him before the foundation of the world... in love he predestined us for adoption." Christ is the One in whom the primordial election of love is expressed; Israel's election was the historical enactment of a love that pre-existed all of history.
9: "The LORD your God is God — the faithful God who keeps his covenant and steadfast love to a thousand generations." The character statement: el ne'eman, the faithful God. Christ embodies this faithfulness — Hebrews 13:8: "Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever." He is the guarantor of the better covenant (Hebrews 7:22): where the Mosaic covenant's longevity depended on human faithfulness, the new covenant is secured by Christ's unfailing faithfulness on behalf of his people.
10: "He repays those who hate him to their face by destroying them." The judicial face of the faithful God — covenant love includes covenant wrath against those who reject it. The same Christ who is "the Lamb" is also the rider on the white horse (Revelation 6:16–17; Deuteronomy 19:11–16). Grace and judgment are not opposites but two expressions of absolute holiness — one for those who trust, one for those who reject.
11: "Therefore keep the commandments, statutes, and rules that I am commanding you today." The imperative follows from the indicative of YHWH's character (v9–10). The NT structure is identical: Ephesians 4:1 — "I urge you to walk worthy of the calling to which you have been called" — follows three chapters of indicative gospel. The election and faithfulness of God grounds the call to faithfulness, in both testaments.
12: "If you heed these ordinances... the LORD your God will maintain his covenant and steadfast love." The Mosaic covenant's conditionality: obedience triggers the covenant blessing. Hebrews 8:7–13 contrasts this with the new covenant: "if that first covenant had been faultless, there would have been no occasion to look for a second." The conditionality of Deuteronomy 7:12 points by contrast to the new covenant's unconditionality — secured by Christ who fulfilled the obedience condition on behalf of his people (Romans 5:19).
13: The blessing catalogue — fruit of womb, ground, grain, wine, oil, cattle, sheep — covers the whole of agricultural life. The new covenant blessings are first spiritual: "every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ" (Ephesians 1:3), and ultimately extended to all creation (Romans 8:19–23). The land-blessings are a partial, temporal preview of the comprehensive renewal Christ inaugurates — first in his resurrection body, ultimately in the new creation.
14: "You will be blessed more than any other people; none of your men or women will be childless." The covenant promise of fruitfulness — the reversal of barrenness. The pattern of miraculous birth to barren women (Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, Elizabeth) reaches its apex in the virginal conception of Christ himself. His coming reverses spiritual barrenness: "the barren one has borne seven children" (1 Samuel 2:5, cited in Galatians 4:27) — covenant-fruitfulness fulfilled in the growth of the church through the gospel.
15: "The LORD will keep you free from every sickness and will not inflict on you the dreadful diseases of Egypt." The covenant health promise. Matthew 8:17 cites Isaiah 53:4 as the ground: "he took our infirmities and bore our diseases." Christ's miracles of healing are the inbreaking of the new covenant's wholeness-promise — previews of the full restoration of shalom (Revelation 21:4: "no more death or mourning or crying or pain").
16: "You must destroy all the peoples the LORD your God hands over to you. Do not look on them with pity." The hardness of the conquest command is precisely the reason the NT shifts the warfare metaphor from military to spiritual: "we do not wrestle against flesh and blood" (Ephesians 6:12). Christ teaches love of enemies (Matthew 5:44); the spiritual enemy is what must be overcome, and the weapons are the gospel and prayer, not swords (2 Corinthians 10:4).
17: "If you say to yourself, 'These nations are more powerful than I am — how can I drive them out?'" — the internal monologue of intimidation. Christ addresses this inner voice: "in the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world" (John 16:33). The "how can I?" question is answered not by an infusion of human strength but by the completed victory of Christ in which believers share (1 Corinthians 15:57; Romans 8:37).
18: "Remember carefully what the LORD your God did to Pharaoh and to all Egypt." Memory as the antidote to fear — recalling past divine action dissolves present terror. The Christian's memory of the cross and resurrection serves the same function: "he who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all — how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?" (Romans 8:32). The cross is the definitive past act that grounds present confidence.
19: "The great ordeals your eyes witnessed, the signs and wonders, the mighty hand and outstretched arm." The Exodus vocabulary of divine power becomes in the NT the vocabulary of resurrection power: "the immeasurable greatness of his power toward us who believe, according to the working of his great might that he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead" (Ephesians 1:19–20). The same "great power" now operates in the resurrection of Christ and the transformation of those who are in him.
20: "The LORD your God will send the hornet among them until those who survive and hide from you have also perished." The supernatural element of the conquest — YHWH acting in advance of Israel's military action. The Spirit goes before in the same way in the gospel advance: opening hearts (Acts 16:14: "the Lord opened her heart"), creating the conditions for the word to take root before the messenger arrives. The gospel advances because the Spirit is always ahead of it.
21: "Do not be terrified by them, for the LORD your God is among you — a great and awesome God." The divine presence is the antidote to terror. In the new covenant, Christ's promise "I am with you always, to the end of the age" (Matthew 28:20) is the new-covenant form of "the LORD your God is among you." His presence through the Spirit he sent (John 14:16–17) makes the church's continuing mission possible against forces that would otherwise overwhelm it.
22: "The LORD your God will drive out these nations gradually, little by little. You must not eliminate them all at once, or the wild animals will multiply against you." The wisdom of gradual advance. Christ's kingdom likewise grows gradually — the parables of the mustard seed and leaven (Matthew 13:31–33) describe a slow, organic process, not overnight conquest. The patient incremental advance of the gospel through history is not a sign of weakness but of God's design for how the kingdom grows.
23: "The LORD your God will hand them over to you and throw them into great panic until they are destroyed." The certainty of final victory despite the gradual process. Paul applies this eschatological certainty to the church: "in all these things we are more than conquerors through him who loved us" (Romans 8:37). Christ's resurrection is the guarantee of the final conquest — the firstfruits of the completed victory (1 Corinthians 15:20–28). The outcome is not in doubt.
24: "He will hand their kings over to you, and you will erase their name from under heaven. No one will be able to stand against you." The comprehensive defeat of every opposing power. Christ's session at the right hand of the Father (Psalms 110:1; Hebrews 1:13) is the current phase of this final defeat: "he must reign until he has put all enemies under his feet" (1 Corinthians 15:25). The earthly kings of Canaan are the type; every power opposed to God's kingdom is the antitype, already positionally defeated and awaiting final judgment.
25: "Burn the carved images of their gods. Do not covet the silver or gold on them or take any of it for yourself, lest you be ensnared by it — it is an abomination to the LORD your God." The contamination warning: even the precious metal coating an idol is tainted. Paul applies the principle in 1 Corinthians 10:20–21. Christ was "made sin" (2 Corinthians 5:21) without being contaminated by it — the one who entered the domain of sin and death and came out uncontaminated by virtue of his sinless nature (Hebrews 4:15).
26: "Do not bring a detestable thing into your house, or you will be devoted to destruction along with it." The ban extends to covetousness — taking the devoted thing contaminates the taker (cf. Achan, Josh 7). Christ cleansed the temple — his Father's house — by driving out the sellers (John 2:13–17), fulfilling the principle that YHWH's house must be free of what is devoted to destruction. His zeal for the Father's house consumed him (John 2:17, citing Psalms 69:9): the dedication to purity the conquest command required, displayed in bodily form.
